examples.title: 2D Composite Transformation Calculator
examples.description: Computes composite transformation matrix by multiplying individual transformation matrices
examples.code:
// 3×3 Matrix Multiplication for 2D Transformations
FUNCTION multiplyMatrices(A, B):
 result ← matrix[3][3]
 FOR i ← 0 TO 2:
 FOR j ← 0 TO 2:
 result[i][j] ← 0
 FOR k ← 0 TO 2:
 result[i][j] ← result[i][j] + A[i][k] × B[k][j]
 RETURN result

// Rotate about arbitrary point (h, k)
FUNCTION rotateAboutPoint(h, k, angle):
 T1 ← translationMatrix(-h, -k)
 R ← rotationMatrix(angle)
 T2 ← translationMatrix(h, k)
 
 temp ← multiplyMatrices(T2, R)
 composite ← multiplyMatrices(temp, T1)
 RETURN composite

// Apply transformation to point
FUNCTION transformPoint(matrix M, point P):
 x' ← M[0][0]×P.x + M[0][1]×P.y + M[0][2]
 y' ← M[1][0]×P.x + M[1][1]×P.y + M[1][2]
 RETURN point(x', y')
examples.explanation: Shows matrix multiplication for combining transformations and applying to points. Demonstrates the three-step process for rotation about arbitrary point.

examples.title: 3D Transformation Pipeline
examples.description: Implements a 3D transformation pipeline with translation, rotation, and scaling using 4×4 homogeneous matrices
examples.code:
// 4×4 Identity Matrix
FUNCTION identityMatrix():
 M ← zeroMatrix(4, 4)
 FOR i ← 0 TO 3:
 M[i][i] ← 1
 RETURN M

// 3D Translation Matrix
FUNCTION translationMatrix3D(tx, ty, tz):
 M ← identityMatrix()
 M[3][0] ← tx
 M[3][1] ← ty
 M[3][2] ← tz
 RETURN M

// 3D Rotation about Z-axis (similar for X, Y)
FUNCTION rotationZMatrix(angle):
 M ← identityMatrix()
 M[0][0] ← cos(angle)
 M[0][1] ← -sin(angle)
 M[1][0] ← sin(angle)
 M[1][1] ← cos(angle)
 RETURN M

// 3D Scaling Matrix
FUNCTION scalingMatrix3D(sx, sy, sz):
 M ← identityMatrix()
 M[0][0] ← sx
 M[1][1] ← sy
 M[2][2] ← sz
 RETURN M

// Apply Complete Pipeline
FUNCTION transformPoint3D(point P, order[]):
 M ← identityMatrix()
 FOR each transform IN order:
 M ← multiplyMatrices4x4(transform.matrix, M)
 RETURN applyMatrix(M, P)
examples.explanation: Demonstrates 3D transformation matrices using homogeneous coordinates. Shows how to build complex transformations by multiplying in specified order.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per point after composite matrix computed

examples.title: Inverse Transformation Calculator
examples.description: Calculates inverse of composite transformation matrices
examples.code:
// Inverse of Basic Transformations
FUNCTION inverseTranslation(tx, ty):
 RETURN translationMatrix(-tx, -ty)

FUNCTION inverseRotation(angle):
 RETURN rotationMatrix(-angle)

FUNCTION inverseScaling(sx, sy):
 RETURN scalingMatrix(1/sx, 1/sy)

// Inverse of Composite Transformation
FUNCTION inverseComposite(M):
 // For M = A × B × C, M⁻¹ = C⁻¹ × B⁻¹ × A⁻¹
 // Reverse the order of inverses
 inverses ← reverse(orderOfTransformations)
 result ← identityMatrix()
 FOR each inv IN inverses:
 result ← multiplyMatrices(result, inv)
 RETURN result

// Alternative: Direct 2×2 inverse for rotation/scale
FUNCTION inverse2x2(M):
 det ← M[0][0]×M[1][1] - M[0][1]×M[1][0]
 RETURN [M[1][1]/det, -M[0][1]/det; 
 -M[1][0]/det, M[0][0]/det]
examples.explanation: Shows how to compute inverses of transformation matrices. Key insight: reverse the order of transformations when inverting a composite matrix.
